name: Chains of the Trandoshan
description: Kos tracks a lead to an old Trandoshan slaving outpost in the Kashyyyk system fringe — a contact who owes him information about a missing shipment. But when he arrives, he finds more than a business deal waiting: Duros and Wookiee captives in shock cages, a child staring at him through the bars, and a Trandoshan boss named Grashk who deals in lives the way other people deal in credits.
hopePole: The Unchained — break every cage, even the ones you can't see, even when it costs everything.
tollPole: The Transaction — get what you came for, walk away clean, and tell yourself it isn't your problem.
setup: The coordinates led you to a hollowed-out asteroid in the trailing debris field of Kashyyyk's third moon. Vel Drenn — the fixer who brokered this meet — warned you the contact would be difficult. He didn't mention the cages. Your ship settles on the landing pad and the first thing you see through the viewport is a row of shock cages stacked three high in the loading bay. Duros. Wookiees. Some too young to stand. A Trandoshan enforcer with a shock prod is walking the line, and behind him, through a blast door, you can hear the low growl of something large and unhappy. Your contact is inside. So are at least thirty captives. Vel Drenn's voice crackles over comms: 'Get in, get the data, get out. Don't be a hero, Kos. Heroes don't come back from places like this.'

rounds.prompt: You step off the ramp into the stink of ozone and fear. The loading bay is lit by flickering work lights, and the shock cages hum with low-voltage current — enough to keep the captives docile, not enough to kill. A young Duros girl, maybe ten years old, presses her face against the bars and watches you with enormous red eyes. She doesn't cry. She doesn't beg. She just watches, like she's memorizing your face for later — for testimony, or for a ghost story. The Trandoshan enforcer blocks your path. 'Business or browsing?' he rasps.
rounds.narrativeContext: The girl's name is Nila. You don't know that yet. Behind her cage, a massive Wookiee female — Khyyra, a former Kashyyyk militia soldier — crouches with three Wookiee children pressed against her. She meets your eyes once, measures you, and looks away. She's been measured by visitors before. None of them helped. Your contact is somewhere past the blast door, in the inner compound where Grashk conducts his real business.

choices.label: I look at the cages and say loud enough for everyone to hear: 'I'm here to discuss inventory. All of it. Take me to Grashk.'
choices.narration: The enforcer's eyes narrow. Nobody asks for Grashk by name unless they have credits or a death wish. But something in your voice — the steadiness, the controlled fury hiding behind commerce — makes him step aside. As you pass the cages, Nila's hand shoots through the bars and grabs your sleeve. Just for a second. Then she lets go. You feel her fingerprints on your wrist all the way to the blast door. Grashk is waiting in a chamber that smells like tanned hide and spice. He's enormous — even for a Trandoshan — and he's smiling the way predators smile when dinner walks itself to the table.

choices.label: I tell the enforcer I'm here on Vel Drenn's referral for a private transaction, and I need to inspect the merchandise before I commit.
choices.narration: Vel Drenn's name buys you passage. The enforcer grunts and waves you through. You walk the line of cages like a buyer — hands behind your back, eyes clinical. It's the hardest performance of your life. Nila watches you pass. Khyyra doesn't even look up. You've been sorted into a category already: another customer, another pair of eyes that sees product instead of people. Your contact is in the back pens — a Rodian data broker named Fash who got caught skimming from Grashk's ledger. He's alive, barely. He has what you need. The question is what it costs to walk him out.

choices.label: I keep my head down, avoid the cages, and slip past the enforcer toward the inner compound — I'm here for one person, not a crusade.
choices.narration: You move through the loading bay like smoke. The enforcer barely registers you — just another shadow in a place full of them. You don't look at the cages. You don't look at Nila. You don't look at the Wookiee children pressed against their mother's fur. You look straight ahead and you keep walking. The blast door is unlocked. Sloppy. Grashk's operation runs on fear, not security. You find Fash in a maintenance alcove, chained to a pipe. He sees you and his eyes flood with desperate hope. 'Get me out,' he whispers. 'I have everything — the shipping manifests, the routes, all of it. Just get me out.' Behind you, through the walls, you can hear a child coughing.

rounds.prompt: Grashk leans back in his throne — literally, a chair made from Wookiee bowcaster stock — and considers you. 'You want to discuss all my inventory,' he says, tasting the words. 'That takes credits. Big credits. Or something worth more than credits.' He slides a datapad across the table. It shows a bounty — your bounty. Someone out there is looking for a certain ghost who flies too fast and asks too many questions. 'Work for me,' Grashk says. 'One run. Deliver a shipment to the Zygerrian consortium. In return, I release ten of your choice, and your bounty disappears.' The shipment, you realize, is people. He wants you to deliver slaves to buy slaves.
rounds.narrativeContext: Grashk is testing you. He already knows you're not a real buyer — your hands are too clean, your eyes too angry. But he's a businessman, and anger is just another commodity if you know how to price it. The ten he's offering would include Nila. Khyyra and the Wookiees aren't on the table — they're too valuable. The Zygerrian run would take three days. The captives in the cages have maybe four days of water left. Your contact Fash is somewhere in this compound, and every minute you spend negotiating is a minute he might not survive.

choices.label: I lean forward and tell Grashk the only shipment leaving this rock is every living soul in those cages — and he can walk away rich or not walk away at all.
choices.narration: Grashk laughs. Then he stops laughing, because your hand is on your blaster and your eyes are the eyes of someone who has already calculated the shot. The room goes very quiet. Two enforcers shift behind you. Grashk's claws tap the armrest. 'You're either very brave or very stupid,' he says. You tell him you're neither — you're the one person in this room who has nothing left to lose. It's a bluff, partly. But Grashk has survived this long by reading people, and what he reads in your face makes him reach for his comlink instead of his weapon.

choices.label: I counter-offer: I'll do one job for him — not a slave run, a cargo run, something clean — in exchange for the Duros children and my contact Fash.
choices.narration: Grashk considers. A clean cargo run means less risk for him, less Imperial attention. And the Duros children are low-value stock — too young to work, too fragile for the fighting pits. 'The children and your Rodian,' he agrees. 'But the Wookiees stay. And if your cargo run goes sideways, I sell them all to cover my losses.' He extends a clawed hand. You shake it. His grip could crush your fingers, and he holds just long enough to make sure you know that. Nila and six other Duros children — you can save seven young lives and your contact. Khyyra and her children, the adult Duros, the others — they stay in the cages.

choices.label: I tell Grashk I'll take the Zygerrian run — but my price is Fash and the bounty, not ten captives I can't feed.
choices.narration: Grashk grins. This is the language he speaks. 'Done,' he says, and there's no handshake — just a datapad with coordinates and a hold full of people you're about to deliver into worse darkness than this. Fash comes with you. He's shaking, bleeding from a cut over his eye, but he has the data. The shipment — eighteen souls, mostly Wookiees — loads onto your ship under guard. You don't look at them. They don't look at you. Everyone in this transaction understands exactly what's happening. Grashk waves from the landing pad as you lift off. Behind you, Nila's cage is still humming. You have three days of hyperspace to not think about what you're doing.

rounds.prompt: You're standing in the pens, playing buyer, when Fash catches your eye from his chain. He mouths two words: 'Power grid.' You realize what he's telling you — the shock cages, the blast doors, the security sensors — they all run through a single junction box in the maintenance corridor. One well-placed charge and every cage opens, every door unlocks, every sensor goes blind for ninety seconds. Ninety seconds of chaos. Khyyra sees you looking at the junction box. She straightens up to her full height — seven feet of scarred Wookiee warrior — and you see understanding pass between you. She knows what you're thinking. She's been waiting for someone to think it.
rounds.narrativeContext: Blowing the junction would free everyone — all thirty-plus captives — but it would also alert every enforcer in the compound. There are at least eight armed Trandoshans between the pens and the landing bay. Khyyra could fight — she's a soldier — but the children can't. Nila can't. And Fash is chained separately; freeing him requires getting to his alcove, which is past Grashk's chamber. A cleaner option: you have enough credits to buy Fash out legitimately. Maybe Nila too, if you frame it right. The others would stay. But you'd walk out clean, no alarms, no firefight around children.

choices.label: I find the junction box, set the charge, and signal Khyyra — we free everyone or we don't leave.
choices.narration: You move through the maintenance corridor with your heart in your throat. The charge is a micro-detonator from your ship's repair kit — not designed for this, but enough. You place it, set the timer, and lock eyes with Khyyra through the cage bars. You hold up nine fingers. Then eight. Then seven. She nods once and gathers the Wookiee children behind her. When the charge blows, the world goes white and silent for half a second. Then every cage door swings open and Khyyra roars — a sound that shakes the walls and freezes the nearest enforcer mid-step. Ninety seconds. The clock is running.

choices.label: I approach the lead enforcer and offer to buy Fash and the Duros girl — a quiet transaction, no complications.
choices.narration: Credits change hands. The enforcer doesn't care about a half-dead Rodian and a Duros child — they're inventory write-offs. Fash limps free, clutching his datapad like a life preserver. Nila walks out of her cage on unsteady legs and grabs your hand without asking. Her fingers are cold. Behind you, Khyyra watches you leave with two out of thirty. Her expression is not anger — it's something worse. It's the look of someone confirming what she already knew: that rescue, when it comes, is always partial. Always someone's arithmetic. You have what you came for, plus one small hand holding yours. The others stay.

choices.label: I buy Fash out — he's what I came for. I tell myself the girl and the Wookiees aren't my mission.
choices.narration: You pay the price. Fash stumbles free. You steer him toward the landing bay without looking back. But Nila's eyes follow you — those enormous red Duros eyes that memorized your face the moment you walked in. She doesn't call out. She doesn't beg. She just watches you go, and you feel the weight of being watched by someone who will remember your face forever. Not with hate. With something worse: the quiet understanding that help was standing right there and chose to walk away. Fash is babbling about the data, the manifests, how grateful he is. You barely hear him.

rounds.prompt: You've found Fash. He's chained, bleeding, but alive and clutching a hidden datachip in his mouth — the information you need. You're uncuffing him when Nila appears in the doorway. She escaped her cage — pried the lock with a stolen hydrospanner. She's ten years old and she broke out on her own. Now she's standing there, shaking, holding the hydrospanner like a weapon, and looking at you with eyes that say: 'I found my way out. Now I need yours.' Behind her, down the corridor, you can hear Trandoshan voices. They've noticed the empty cage. You have maybe two minutes before they search this section.
rounds.narrativeContext: Nila is small enough to hide in your ship's smuggling compartments — the same ones you've used to evade Imperial scans. Fash can barely walk. Taking both of them means moving slower through a compound that's about to go on alert. Taking just Fash means leaving a ten-year-old who broke out of a cage by herself to be caught and punished — or worse, made an example of. The landing pad is sixty meters away. Khyyra and the other captives are still in the main pen. You can hear Khyyra's low growl through the walls — she knows something is happening.

choices.label: I take Nila, take Fash, and on the way to the ship I pop the cage locks on every pen I pass — if I'm burning this place, I'm burning it all the way down.
choices.narration: You scoop Nila up with one arm and drag Fash with the other. The hydrospanner she stole fits the cage locks — you figured that out the second you saw it. Every pen you pass, you jam the tool in and twist. Cage after cage swings open. Duros stumble out, blinking. Wookiees rise to their full height. Khyyra is the last cage — she doesn't stumble. She walks out like she's been waiting for this specific moment her entire life, scoops her children under her arms, and looks at you with something you've never seen directed at you before: trust. The compound alarm starts wailing. You have sixty meters, thirty freed captives, and every Trandoshan in the asteroid reaching for a weapon.

choices.label: I take Nila and Fash — she earned her way out when she broke that lock. But the others are behind blast doors I can't open in two minutes.
choices.narration: Nila fits in the smuggling compartment like she was made for it. You seal Fash in the secondary hold and start the pre-flight sequence with shaking hands. Through the viewport, you can see enforcers running toward the empty cage. They haven't connected it to your ship yet — you have seconds, not minutes. Nila's hand finds yours through the compartment gap. She squeezes once. You squeeze back. Then you punch the throttle and the asteroid falls away behind you. In the loading bay, Khyyra hears your engines and looks up. She doesn't roar. She just closes her eyes and holds her children tighter.

choices.label: I take Fash and go — the girl broke out on her own, she can find another way. I can't risk the mission for a child I don't know.
choices.narration: You look at Nila. She looks at you. For a long second, the galaxy holds its breath. Then you grab Fash and move toward the landing bay without her. She doesn't cry. She doesn't scream. She steps back into the shadows of the corridor, still holding the hydrospanner, and you realize she's going to try to hide — a ten-year-old Duros in a compound full of angry Trandoshans, armed with a tool she barely knows how to use. You tell yourself she's resourceful. She broke out once. Maybe she'll break out again. The math doesn't work, and you know it, but you're already running and the engines are warming and Fash has the data and the data is why you came.

rounds.prompt: Grashk reaches for his comlink — but not to call for help. He's calling the Zygerrian consortium. 'Change of plans,' he says. 'The shipment's cancelled.' Then he looks at you. 'You want them all? Fine. But I need something in return that's worth more than thirty slaves to the Zygerrians. I need the Imperial patrol routes for this sector. You're a ghost — you have them, or you know how to get them.' He's right. You have the routes. Giving them to a slaver means he relocates his operation somewhere the Empire can't find him. More captives. More cages. More Nilas. Or you can refuse, and this standoff ends in blaster fire with thirty innocents in the crossfire.
rounds.narrativeContext: The patrol routes would let Grashk move his operation deeper into Wild Space, beyond Imperial reach — which means beyond anyone's reach. He'd keep slaving, but in places where no one can stop him. Refusing means a fight in close quarters surrounded by cages full of children. Khyyra could help, but she's still locked up. You're outgunned, outmanned, and the only thing between those captives and a firefight is your next words.

choices.label: I give him fake routes — modified patrol data that leads straight into an Imperial interdiction zone. He'll relocate into a trap.
choices.outcome: You hand over the datapad with a steady hand and a forger's smile. Grashk checks the data — it looks real because it mostly is, with just enough modification to send his next convoy directly into the teeth of an Imperial patrol. He releases the captives. All of them. Nila walks out holding Khyyra's hand, and the Wookiee soldier looks at you with the first real smile you've seen on this rock. You load thirty-one souls onto your ship and jump to hyperspace before Grashk can verify the routes. Somewhere behind you, in a few weeks, a Trandoshan slaving operation is going to meet an Imperial interdiction cruiser, and neither side is going to enjoy the encounter. You didn't just empty the cages — you set a trap that will crack the operation from the inside. Vel Drenn calls you reckless. Nila calls you something in Durese that translates roughly to 'the one who came back.' Your ship groans under the weight of thirty-one rescued souls, your cover is smoke, and somewhere in the Kashyyyk system, a Trandoshan is about to learn that ghosts leave traps as well as footprints.

choices.label: I give him the real routes — his operation relocates, but these thirty people walk free today.
choices.outcome: You hand over the real data. Every route, every patrol schedule, every gap in Imperial coverage. Grashk reads it with hungry eyes and waves a claw. The cages open. Thirty people stumble into the light, blinking, shaking, alive. Nila grabs your hand. Khyyra carries three children and doesn't look back. You load them all and jump. You saved every person in that compound. Every single one. But you also gave a slaver the keys to operate without interference — somewhere in Wild Space, Grashk is building new cages right now, and the people who'll fill them have no idea that their suffering was the price of thirty strangers' freedom. Vel Drenn tells you it was the smart play. You tell him to shut up. At night, in hyperspace, you hear Nila singing a Duros lullaby to the other children. It's the most beautiful and terrible sound you've ever heard, because you know it was bought with routes that lead to other cages, other children, other songs that will never be sung.

choices.label: I draw on Grashk and take my chances — no deals with slavers, no data exchanges, just blasterfire and consequences.
choices.outcome: Your blaster clears the holster before Grashk's claws reach his weapon. The first shot takes out the comlink. The second sends the enforcers scrambling. Grashk flips the table and the room becomes a kill box — blaster bolts and claws and the screaming of everyone in earshot. You fight your way back to the pens, but by the time you reach them, the enforcers have locked down the cages with emergency protocols. You can hear the children screaming behind electrified bars. You grab Fash from the chaos, drag him to the ship, and punch through the asteroid's docking clamp at full thrust. Behind you, the compound is on fire and the cages are locked and Nila is pressing her face against the bars watching your engine glow shrink into nothing. You got what you came for. Your contact. Your data. Your pride. You didn't give an inch to a slaver. Vel Drenn says you did the principled thing. The principled thing tastes like ash and sounds like children's voices, muffled by blast doors, calling for help that's already gone.

rounds.prompt: The deal is struck — Grashk gives you the Duros children and Fash in exchange for a clean cargo run. You're loading seven children and a half-dead Rodian onto your ship when Khyyra catches your eye through the cage bars. She makes a gesture in Shyriiwook hand-sign that Kos recognizes from the spacer docks: 'I can fight. Open my cage and I will handle the rest.' Behind her, three Wookiee children cling to her legs. Opening Khyyra's cage means breaking the deal — and when you break deals with Trandoshans, nobody walks away clean.
rounds.narrativeContext: Grashk is watching from the blast door. His enforcers are positioned between the pens and your ship. The cargo run he wants is legitimate — power converters to a mining colony, nothing illegal. But his operation continues. Khyyra stays. The adult Duros stay. Opening that cage means war, and war means the seven children you already saved are back in danger. Khyyra knows this. She's asking anyway.

choices.label: I pop Khyyra's cage and tell the children to run for the ship — we fight our way out together or not at all.
choices.outcome: The lock breaks. Khyyra erupts from the cage like a force of nature, and the first enforcer goes down before he can raise his weapon. You put the children behind you, Fash behind them, and Khyyra in front of you, and you fight through the loading bay with blaster fire painting the walls. Nila runs beside you, clutching a younger child's hand, not screaming, not crying, just running with the focused determination of someone who decided to survive. Grashk roars behind you — a deal broken, a profit lost, a dangerous enemy made. You don't care. Khyyra loads her children first, then turns and goes back in. She comes out carrying two more Duros adults and dragging an enforcer by his ankle. Your ship lifts off overloaded, sparking, one engine coughing smoke, and every soul in the compound who could run is on board. Not everyone made it. Three Duros are still in the deep pens — the blast doors sealed before Khyyra could reach them. But twenty-three people breathe free air tonight because a Wookiee soldier asked and a ghost said yes.

choices.label: I sign back to Khyyra: 'Not today. But I'm coming back.' Then I load the children and honor the deal.
choices.outcome: Khyyra's hand drops. She nods once — a soldier accepting a tactical decision, not a moral one. You load the seven children, Fash, and yourself onto the ship. Nila sits in the co-pilot seat because she refuses to go in the hold, and she watches the asteroid shrink through the viewport with those enormous eyes. 'Are you coming back?' she asks. 'Yes,' you say. You mean it. You do Grashk's cargo run in two days, clean and professional. Then you spend a week planning. You pull in every favor, every contact, every credit you have. When you go back for Khyyra, you don't go alone — you bring a crew, a plan, and enough firepower to crack the compound wide open. But that's later. Tonight, seven children sleep in your cargo hold, and Nila's hand finds yours in the dark and squeezes, and you hold on like she's an anchor in a sea that never stops moving.

choices.label: I look away from Khyyra and seal my hatch — the deal keeps these children alive, and I won't gamble them on a jailbreak.
choices.outcome: You don't sign back. You turn away from Khyyra's eyes and close the ramp. The seal hisses shut and Khyyra's face disappears behind durasteel, and you tell yourself this is survival arithmetic — seven saved versus zero saved if the deal breaks and the shooting starts. The cargo run goes smoothly. Grashk is satisfied. The seven Duros children settle into a refugee camp on Lothal, and Fash gives you the data you need. Mission complete. But for months afterward, you dream about Khyyra's hand-sign: 'I can fight.' Two words in gesture that mean: 'I am still here. I am still strong. I am still waiting for someone who sees me.' You saw her. You walked away. Nila asks about the Wookiee lady sometimes. You change the subject. Eventually she stops asking, and that silence is louder than any question.

rounds.prompt: You're in hyperspace with a hold full of slaves destined for the Zygerrian consortium. Eighteen souls. Fash is in the cockpit, patching his wounds and feeding you the data you came for. The shipping manifests, the routes, the contacts — everything you need. Through the cargo hold speakers, you can hear them — quiet conversations in Shyriiwook, a child humming, someone praying. The Zygerrian drop point is sixteen hours away. In sixteen hours, these people become property. Your navicomputer shows an alternative course: a rebel sympathizer station that takes refugees, no questions asked. It would mean burning your identity, losing the bounty protection Grashk promised, and making an enemy of both the Trandoshans and the Zygerrians.
rounds.narrativeContext: Fash doesn't know about the alternative course. He's just happy to be alive. The data he's giving you is worth a fortune — enough to set you up for years if you sell it right. Delivering the slaves completes the deal cleanly. Diverting means war with two criminal organizations simultaneously. The Wookiees in your hold don't know they're being delivered into slavery — they think you're a rescue ship. The child humming is singing a lullaby her mother taught her on Kashyyyk before the slavers came.

choices.label: I change course for the rebel station — I took this run to get Fash out, not to deliver people into chains. The deal dies here.
choices.outcome: You pull the lever and the stars shift. Fash looks up from his datapad. 'That's not the Zygerrian course,' he says. 'No,' you say. 'It's not.' The rebel station is a converted freighter hidden in a nebula. They take the Wookiees without questions, without judgment. The child who was humming — her name is Taasha — runs down the ramp and turns back to look at you. She waves. It's such a small, ordinary gesture, and it cracks something open in your chest that you didn't know was sealed. Grashk's bounty protection is gone. The Zygerrians will hunt you. The Trandoshans will hunt you. Your identity is burned, your contacts are poisoned, and Vel Drenn will never broker for you again. But eighteen people are free tonight because you remembered, somewhere in hyperspace between one life and another, that the ghost still has a conscience. Fash stares at you like you've lost your mind. Maybe you have. Maybe losing your mind is the most human thing you've done in years.

choices.label: I stage an 'engine failure' that forces an emergency drop — the Wookiees 'escape' during the breakdown, and I report the loss to the Zygerrians as mechanical failure.
choices.outcome: You rig the hyperdrive to throw a fault code and drop out of hyperspace near a habitable moon. Then you 'accidentally' leave the cargo hold unsealed during 'repairs.' The Wookiees are warriors — they figure it out fast. When the hold door opens, they don't run. They walk out with dignity, carrying their children, and the last one out — an old male with silver-streaked fur — turns and places his hand flat against the bulkhead. A Wookiee gesture of respect. Then they're gone, into the forest of a moon that has no name on any Imperial chart. You seal the hold, 'fix' the engine, and report the loss to the Zygerrians. They're suspicious but can't prove sabotage. Grashk is furious but can't risk an investigation. You kept your cover, mostly. You kept the bounty protection, mostly. And somewhere on a nameless moon, eighteen Wookiees are free because a ghost had an engine problem at exactly the right time. It's not heroism. It's plausible deniability with a conscience.

choices.label: I complete the delivery — these people were slaves before I got here, and they'll be slaves after. At least Fash is free and I get what I need.
choices.outcome: The Zygerrian station looms in the viewport like a jaw. You dock, the cargo doors open, and the Wookiees file out in chains that weren't there when you loaded them — the Zygerrians brought their own. The child — Taasha — looks back at you one last time. She doesn't wave. She just looks at you with eyes that are learning, right now, in this moment, that the galaxy has a specific kind of cruelty: the kind that wears a human face and calls itself necessity. Grashk is pleased. Your bounty disappears. Fash gives you the data, all of it, and you sell it for enough credits to disappear for a year. Clean. Efficient. Ghost protocol. But in the quiet hours, you hear a child humming a Kashyyyk lullaby through cargo hold speakers that are turned off, and you know that sound will follow you to every port, every safehouse, every anonymous berth where you try to sleep without dreaming. You completed the transaction. The math works. The child's face doesn't.

rounds.prompt: The cages are open and chaos reigns. Khyyra is fighting through enforcers like a siege engine, but the blast door to the landing bay is sealed — someone hit the lockdown. You have sixty seconds of sensor blindness left, thirty-plus freed captives stampeding through corridors they don't know, and Trandoshan enforcers regrouping at every junction. Fash grabs your arm: 'The ventilation system — it connects to the landing bay. But it's a one-person crawl. Someone has to go through, override the door from the other side, and hold it open while everyone runs.' Nila is already looking at the vent. She's the smallest person here. She's ten.
rounds.narrativeContext: Khyyra can hold the corridor for another minute, maybe two. The ventilation shaft is too small for any adult. Nila broke out of a cage by herself — she's resourceful, brave, and terrified. Sending a child through a ventilation shaft in a compound full of armed slavers is unconscionable. Not sending anyone means everyone stays trapped until the enforcers regroup and the cages slam shut again. There might be another way — you could blow the blast door with your last detonator, but the explosion in these close quarters could kill anyone standing near it.

choices.label: I boost Nila into the vent and tell her exactly what to do — she's the bravest person on this rock and I trust her to open that door.
choices.outcome: Nila doesn't hesitate. She climbs into the vent like water flowing uphill, her small body disappearing into the metal throat of the station. You can hear her moving — quick, determined, a child crawling through darkness to save thirty people she doesn't know. Khyyra roars behind you, holding the line. You count seconds. Forty-five. Thirty. Fifteen. The blast door shudders, groans, and opens. Nila is standing on the other side, her hands bleeding from the override panel, her enormous red eyes bright with something that looks like pride. Everyone runs. Khyyra brings up the rear, carrying wounded, her fur singed from blaster burns. Your ship lifts off with thirty-one souls and a ten-year-old hero who jimmied the lock on freedom with bleeding hands and zero hesitation. Grashk's compound burns behind you — Khyyra set the fuel reserves before she left. The stars stretch into hyperspace lines and Nila falls asleep in the co-pilot seat with her head against your arm. You'll carry her fingerprints on your wrist for the rest of your life. Both sets — the ones from the cage bars, and the ones from the blast door panel. Both made of the same thing: a child's refusal to accept that cages are forever.

choices.label: I use the last detonator on the blast door — it's dangerous, but I'm not sending a child through a vent shaft in a war zone.
choices.outcome: The detonator tears the blast door apart. The concussion throws you backward and your ears ring for days afterward. Two of the freed captives catch shrapnel — nothing fatal, but enough blood to make the corridor look like a battlefield. Khyyra pushes through the wreckage first, shielding her children with her body. Nila runs past you without flinching — this child has been desensitized to explosions, and that thought is worse than the blast itself. You load everyone you can reach onto the ship. Twenty-six out of thirty-one make it. Five were cut off when the secondary blast doors sealed — three Duros adults and two Wookiee elders who couldn't run fast enough. Your ship limps into hyperspace trailing smoke and hope. Twenty-six people breathe free air tonight. Five don't. Nila asks about the ones who didn't make it. You tell her the truth. She nods like a grown-up and goes quiet for three hours. When she speaks again, she asks if you can teach her to fly. You say yes. It's the only answer that doesn't feel like a lie.

choices.label: I grab Nila, grab Fash, and fight our way to the landing bay through the side corridor — everyone else follows Khyyra or they don't follow anyone.
choices.outcome: You cut a path through the side corridor with blaster fire and desperation. Nila under one arm, Fash dragging behind you. Khyyra sees you go and makes her choice — she rallies the strongest captives and charges the blast door with raw Wookiee force. The door buckles. Some get through. Some don't. You don't count. You reach your ship, throw Nila and Fash aboard, and lift off while the loading bay is still a war zone. Through the viewport, you see Khyyra — she made it to the bay, her children behind her, but your ramp is already closing. She watches your ship rise and there is no accusation in her eyes. Only the steady recognition of a soldier who understands triage. Fourteen people made it out behind your trail. The other seventeen didn't. Nila stares out the viewport as the asteroid shrinks. 'The big one,' she says. 'The Wookiee with the children.' You tell her Khyyra made it. You don't know if that's true. You fly for six hours in silence, carrying the weight of a rescue that felt more like a retreat.

rounds.prompt: You're clear of the compound with Nila, Fash, and your conscience doing complicated math. You jump to hyperspace and Fash starts unpacking the data — the shipping manifests, the slave routes, the buyer lists. Then he goes quiet. 'Kos,' he says. 'These routes — there's a shipment scheduled in four days. Forty-eight captives from a Wookiee village. Kashyyyk deep forest. The pickup coordinates are right here.' He holds up the datapad. You have four days, exact coordinates, and the element of surprise. You could intercept the slavers before they reach the village. Or you could sell the data — the routes alone are worth enough credits to disappear for two years.
rounds.narrativeContext: Nila is asleep in the cargo hold, curled around the hydrospanner she used to break out of her cage. She hasn't let go of it since you left. The Wookiee village on Fash's datapad is deep in Imperial-controlled territory. Going there means threading Imperial patrols, dodging Trandoshan scouts, and arriving at a village that has no idea slavers are coming. Selling the data means someone else might act on it — or might not. You've already saved two people today. The question is whether today's math is finished.

choices.label: I set course for Kashyyyk — four days is enough to warn that village and set up an ambush. Nila sleeps while I plot the intercept.
choices.outcome: The wroshyr trees of Kashyyyk have a smell that hits you through the landing ramp — green and ancient and alive in a way that space stations never are. The village elder doesn't believe you at first. Then Nila walks down the ramp, a Duros child with cage marks on her wrists, and the elder's doubt dies on his face. They believe her. They prepare. When the slavers come three days later, they find a village that's not sleeping — crossbow-armed Wookiees in every tree, traps on every path, and a ghost's ship providing air cover with improvised targeting. The slavers retreat. Forty-eight Wookiees stay free. Nila watches the battle from the ship with wide eyes and quiet intensity, and when it's over she looks at you and says, in careful Basic: 'Again.' She means: do it again. Find the next one. Save them too. You look at her and you understand that you've become something you never planned to be: not a ghost, but a light that other people follow. The math stopped working three days ago. You're operating on something else now — something that doesn't calculate and doesn't stop.

choices.label: I transmit the route data anonymously to a rebel cell I know — let people with armies handle armies. I've done my part.
choices.outcome: The transmission goes out on a frequency you haven't used in years. An old rebel contact — someone from the Clone Wars days who never stopped fighting. You don't sign it. You don't wait for a reply. You just send the coordinates, the timing, and a single word: 'Slavers.' Then you set course for the outer rim with Nila and Fash. Maybe the rebels act on it. Maybe they don't. Maybe forty-eight Wookiees live free because of one anonymous transmission, or maybe they don't. You'll never know. Nila wakes up when you drop out of hyperspace at a refugee station. She looks out the viewport and asks where you're going next. 'Somewhere safe,' you tell her. She nods, but her eyes say she doesn't believe in safe anymore — she's ten years old and she's already learned that safe is a thing other people have. You settle Fash at the station, sell the non-route data for enough to keep flying, and keep Nila because she won't let go of your hand and you can't make yourself uncurl her fingers. The ghost has a passenger now. The math is different when there's someone small in the co-pilot seat.

choices.label: I sell the route data to a competing syndicate — let the criminals eat each other. I take the credits and set Nila up somewhere safe.
choices.outcome: The data sells for more than you expected. Enough credits to buy Nila a place at a Duros foster collective on Duro — legitimate, safe, legal. You drop her at the door with a bag of credits, a new identity chip, and the hydrospanner she won't let go of. She looks at you when you turn to leave. 'You're not staying,' she says. It's not a question. 'No,' you say. 'But you're safe.' She nods. Then: 'The Wookiee lady. Khyyra. The children in the other cages. Are they safe too?' You don't answer. You can't. Because the data you sold will start a gang war in the Kashyyyk system, and in gang wars, the captives are always the first casualties. The competing syndicate won't rescue those Wookiees — they'll steal them. Different cages, same chains. Nila's foster collective sends you updates for a while. She's adjusting. She's quiet. She still sleeps with the hydrospanner. You read the updates in safe houses and anonymous berths and you tell yourself she's better off. She probably is. That doesn't make the silence in your cockpit any less loud.

rounds.prompt: You're in hyperspace with Fash and the data. Clean extraction, minimal exposure. Exactly what Vel Drenn ordered. Then Fash, recovering in the medbay, says something that stops you cold: 'You know why Grashk let me keep the datachip? Because it has his insurance on it. His dead man's switch. If anything happens to him, that chip broadcasts the locations of every slave cache he operates — six compounds, four systems, two hundred captives.' He pauses. 'If Grashk dies or gets arrested, those locations go public and every competitor raids those caches. The captives become collateral damage.' He looks at you. 'Destroy the chip and the captives stay hidden but alive. Keep it and you have the power to shut down his whole operation — but if Grashk ever finds out you have it, he triggers the switch remotely and those people become bargaining chips in a war zone.'
rounds.narrativeContext: The datachip is in your hand. It contains everything: the data you came for, plus Grashk's insurance — the locations of two hundred captives across four systems. Destroying the chip means losing the data you came for AND protecting two hundred lives from becoming collateral. Keeping it means you hold the most dangerous piece of information in the sector. Using it means risking a cascade that turns those compounds into war zones. And somewhere back on that asteroid, Nila is still in her cage, and Khyyra is still counting the hours, and the galaxy is exactly as dark as you found it.

choices.label: I copy the data I need, then I destroy the chip and the insurance file. Two hundred people stay safe in their cages — but they stay alive.
choices.outcome: You strip the chip down to the shipping data you need. Then you put the insurance file — the locations, the dead man's switch, the two hundred lives encoded in ones and zeroes — into the trash compactor. The chip crackles, sparks, and dies. Fash watches you do it with his mouth open. 'That was worth a fortune,' he says. 'That was worth two hundred people,' you say. The data you kept is enough to complete your original mission. Not enough to be a hero. Not enough to burn Grashk's empire down. But those six compounds — those four systems — they stay quiet. Two hundred captives stay in their cages, alive, until someone with more resources and fewer ghosts to chase comes along to free them. You couldn't save them. You couldn't even save Nila. But you made sure that your mission didn't cost them their lives, and in a galaxy where mathematics and morality run in opposite directions, that's the only equation that balanced. Fash calls you a fool. You call Vel Drenn and tell him the job's done. Neither of them understands what the chip cost you. Neither of them has to.

choices.label: I keep the chip intact and hide it — I don't use it, don't sell it, don't destroy it. Insurance against the day I go back.
choices.outcome: The chip goes into a hidden compartment in your ship — the same smuggling space that could have held Nila if you'd made a different choice. You don't sell it. You don't share it. You don't destroy it. It sits there, humming with the potential energy of two hundred lives and the knowledge that you hold a weapon you're not ready to use. Fash gives you the data and disappears into the outer rim. Vel Drenn gets his cut. The mission is complete. But the chip stays with you like a second heartbeat, and some nights you pull it out and stare at the encrypted data — six compounds, four systems, two hundred names you'll never know — and you wonder if holding it makes you a guardian or just another kind of jailer. Nila's face appears in your dreams sometimes. The cage bars. The red eyes. The hand that reached for your sleeve. You keep the chip because someday, maybe, you'll be brave enough or desperate enough or angry enough to use it. Until then, it waits. You both wait.

choices.label: I keep the chip, sell copies of the insurance data to Grashk's competitors, and let them tear each other apart for the captive caches.
choices.outcome: You sell the data three times — to three different syndicates, each one thinking they have an exclusive. Within a week, Grashk's dead man's switch triggers not because he's dead but because the market for his captives has become a war zone. Six compounds go up in smoke as competitors raid them. The captives — the two hundred people encrypted on that chip — become exactly what Fash warned you about: collateral damage in a criminal civil war. Some are rescued by the raiders. Some are taken as new inventory. Some don't survive the crossfire. You never learn the exact numbers. You tell yourself the situation was untenable anyway — those people were slaves with or without your interference. The credits from the three sales set you up for years. Fash is horrified. Vel Drenn is impressed. And somewhere in the wreckage of a compound you never visited, a cage that held a child hums with residual charge, and the child is gone, and you don't know where, and you don't look, because the math stops working when you count the faces you can't see.

rounds.prompt: The compound is in chaos. Every cage is open, Khyyra is leading freed Wookiees in a running battle through the corridors, and you're carrying Nila toward the landing bay with Fash stumbling behind you. Then you round the corner and see Grashk himself — massive, armed with a Trandoshan scatter-blaster, standing between you and your ship. Behind him, four enforcers. Behind you, thirty freed captives with nowhere else to go. Grashk's eyes find Nila in your arms. 'That one cost me three hundred credits,' he snarls. 'You're going to cost me a lot more.' Khyyra is forty meters back, fighting. She can't reach you in time.
rounds.narrativeContext: Grashk is a dead shot with that scatter-blaster. In these close quarters, one pull of the trigger would hit you, Nila, and half the people behind you. But he's also a businessman — he won't fire if it means destroying merchandise. The freed captives are still 'product' in his mind. As long as they're alive and undamaged, they're worth something. The moment they're not — the moment this becomes personal instead of financial — the math changes. Your blaster is in your holster. Nila's arms are around your neck.

choices.label: I set Nila down behind me, draw my blaster, and tell Grashk his operation is finished — every life in this compound walks free or neither of us walks out.
choices.outcome: You set Nila down gently. She grabs your leg and doesn't let go. You draw your blaster and point it not at Grashk but at the fuel line running along the ceiling above his head. 'That's your fuel reserve,' you say. 'One shot and this whole bay goes up. Your product, your compound, your ship. All of it.' Grashk's eyes follow your aim. He does the math — he always does the math. Thirty captives minus the entire compound equals nothing. He lowers the scatter-blaster. 'Take them,' he growls. 'But you're dead, ghost. You and every one of them. I'll hunt you across every system in this sector.' 'Get in line,' you say. Thirty-one people board your ship. Nila won't leave your side. Khyyra fights through to the bay with blood on her fur and fire in her eyes and loads the last stragglers with the efficiency of a soldier who has been waiting her entire captivity for this exact moment. Your ship screams into hyperspace overloaded and burning and alive — every soul, every child, every pair of hands that was chained thirty minutes ago now pressing against viewports watching the stars stretch into freedom. Grashk will hunt you. The Empire will hunt you. Everyone will hunt you. But tonight, right now, every cage is empty and Nila is asleep in the co-pilot seat and the ghost has become something the Trandoshans have never seen before: a haunting they can't escape.

choices.label: I hold Nila up where Grashk can see her and offer a trade — the children walk free, and I leave the adults as a compromise.
choices.outcome: Grashk considers. Children are low-margin — too young to work, too fragile for the pits, too much overhead in feeding and guarding. The adults are where the real credits are. 'The children,' he says. 'All of them. And you never come back.' You nod. It's a deal cut in a child's body weight and measured in years of captivity you can't prevent. Nine children — Duros and Wookiee alike — file past Grashk's enforcers and onto your ship. Nila leads them, holding the smallest by the hand. Khyyra watches from the corridor. Her Wookiee children are among the nine. She pushes them forward, then steps back. She's staying. She chose to let her children go and stay behind to protect the adults who remain. The last thing you see before the ramp closes is Khyyra's hand — flat against the blast door, the same gesture of respect the Wookiee elders use when they say goodbye to warriors who won't return. Your ship lifts off with nine children, Fash, and a hole in your chest where certainty used to live. You saved the children. Khyyra saved the children. The adults remain. The math never works — you just learn to live with the remainder.

choices.label: I throw Fash's datachip at Grashk's feet — here's your insurance data, your dead man's switch, everything — and while he's distracted, I grab Nila and run for the ship.
choices.outcome: The datachip clatters across the floor. Grashk's eyes snap to it — the insurance file, his entire leverage system, lying at his feet. In that half-second of distraction, you sprint. Nila pressed against your chest, Fash stumbling behind you, the ramp lowering as you run. You make it. Barely. Blaster bolts score the hull as you lift off, and one clips the landing strut hard enough to make the ship shudder. But you're airborne, and the asteroid is shrinking, and Nila is alive and Fash is alive and you are alive. Behind you, thirty freed captives scatter through the corridors of a compound where the doors are open and the guards are confused and the boss is reading his own insurance file off the floor. Some will escape. Some will be recaptured. Khyyra — you saw her, briefly, through the viewport as you lifted off. She was herding Wookiees toward a secondary bay where a slaver's shuttle sat unguarded. Maybe she made it. Maybe she didn't. You don't know. You chose speed over certainty, survival over completeness, and Nila's breathing against your chest over the fate of everyone else. Vel Drenn calls it a clean extraction. Nila calls it Tuesday. You call it the first day you stopped pretending the math works.

rounds.prompt: You're clear of the asteroid with Nila in the smuggling compartment and Fash in the hold. The jump to hyperspace is clean — no pursuit, no alarms. Then your comm crackles. It's Khyyra. She found a comm unit in the chaos after your departure and she's broadcasting on a frequency she shouldn't know — the old spacer family frequency. The one your mother taught you. 'I heard the child use it,' Khyyra growls in broken Basic. 'She called you on it when you came. I listened. I learned.' A pause. 'The slavers are reorganizing. Two hours before they lock everything down again. My children are here. Please.' Two hours. You could turn around.
rounds.narrativeContext: Nila taught Khyyra the frequency. A ten-year-old sharing the only weapon she had — a sound, a signal, a way to call for help — with a Wookiee soldier she'd known for three days. Turning back means re-entering a compound that's now on full alert. Your ship is small, lightly armed, and carrying two passengers who can't fight. But Khyyra's children are in there. And Khyyra asked. And she asked on your mother's frequency, which means Nila gave her something that was supposed to be sacred, something private, something family — and Nila decided that Khyyra was family.

choices.label: I turn the ship around. Khyyra called on my mother's frequency. That makes her family. You don't leave family in cages.
choices.outcome: The stars reverse as you pull out of hyperspace and wheel back toward the asteroid. Nila's head pops up from the smuggling compartment. 'We're going back?' she asks. 'We're going back,' you say. She nods, no fear, no hesitation, and for a moment you see your mother in this child's face — the same steady refusal to abandon someone who reached out in the dark. The compound is chaos. You come in hot, guns blazing for the first time in years — the ghost is loud tonight. Khyyra is waiting at the secondary bay with her three children and four other Wookiees who rallied when they heard the ship's engines. You lower the ramp at full hover and Khyyra throws her children aboard with a warrior's precision, then leaps after them with a roar that shakes the landing bay. Eight more souls. Your ship protests every gram. The hyperdrive whines at a frequency that means 'one more jump, maybe two, then I'm done.' But you make the jump. You always make the jump. Nila and Khyyra's youngest curl up together in the cargo hold — a Duros child and a Wookiee cub, both cage survivors, both sleeping the deep sleep of people who have been carried to safety by someone who came back. Your mother's frequency. Your mother's lesson. You don't fly past people who are calling.

choices.label: I transmit the asteroid's coordinates and security codes to a rebel frequency I know — someone else can mount the rescue. I can't go back with two passengers and no firepower.
choices.outcome: You key the rebel frequency with numb fingers. Coordinates. Guard rotations. Cage configurations. Everything you observed during your extraction. Then you send a second message on the spacer family frequency, to Khyyra: 'Help is coming. Not me. But help.' You don't know if the rebels will get there in two hours. You don't know if Khyyra received your message. You jump to hyperspace and Nila asks who you were talking to on the comm. 'Friends,' you say. She accepts this with the terrifying pragmatism of a child who has learned not to ask too many questions. Fash patches himself up and doesn't comment on the transmission he overheard. Weeks later, you pick up a rebel dispatch: an unauthorized raid on a Trandoshan slaver compound in the Kashyyyk debris field. Partial success. Eighteen freed, twelve unaccounted for. Khyyra's name isn't mentioned. Her children's names aren't mentioned. You read the dispatch three times, looking for details that aren't there. Nila hears you talking about it and asks: 'The Wookiee lady with the children — did your friends find her?' You tell her you think so. You're lying, and she knows it, and she lets you lie because she's ten years old and she's already learned that sometimes lies are the only kindness adults have left.

choices.label: I switch off the comm and jump. Khyyra used a frequency she had no right to use, and sympathy is a trap I can't afford.
choices.outcome: The comm goes silent. The stars stretch. Nila hears the frequency die and looks at you with those red eyes that see everything and forgive nothing. 'That was the Wookiee lady,' she says. 'Yes,' you say. 'She was calling for help.' 'Yes.' 'And you turned it off.' You don't answer. Nila doesn't speak to you for six hours. When she does, she says: 'I gave her the frequency. I taught it to her. Because I thought you were the kind of person who answers.' The worst part isn't Khyyra's voice, or the children's faces, or the fact that you had the fuel and the time and the coordinates and you chose not to go back. The worst part is Nila — sitting in your co-pilot seat, holding her hydrospanner, looking at you with the devastating clarity of a child who just learned that the person who saved her is not the person she thought. You saved two people today. You had the chance to save more. The ghost made a choice, and the child watched him make it, and the frequency your mother taught you — the one that means 'family in trouble, come home' — went silent because you pressed a button. Fash tells you it was the smart move. Nila tells you nothing. Her silence is louder than any frequency.

rounds.prompt: You're in the corridor, dragging Fash toward the landing bay, and behind you Nila has disappeared into the shadows with her hydrospanner. The enforcers are sweeping the compound — they've found the empty cage, and they're angry. Your ship is sixty meters away. Fash has the data. The mission is complete. Then you hear it: a child's scream, cut short. Not Nila — one of the other children in the cages. The enforcers are taking their frustration out on the captives. Someone is being punished for Nila's escape. Through the wall, you hear a shock prod cycling and a Wookiee howling — Khyyra, trying to shield the children with her body.
rounds.narrativeContext: The sounds coming through the wall are the sounds of cruelty looking for a target. The enforcers can't find Nila, so they're hurting the ones they can reach. Khyyra is taking the punishment for everyone — you can hear her absorbing shock after shock, growling through clenched teeth, refusing to fall. Every second you stay increases your risk. Every second you stay is a second Khyyra endures. Your ship's engines are warm. The landing bay is clear. Fash is pulling at your arm, whispering 'Go, go, go.' And somewhere in the shadows behind you, a ten-year-old Duros girl with a hydrospanner is hiding alone in a compound that just became much more dangerous.

choices.label: I shove Fash toward the ship, turn around, and go back — for Nila, for Khyyra, for every person those enforcers are hurting because of what I started.
choices.outcome: You push Fash through the landing bay door and tell him to start the ship. Then you turn around and run back into the dark. The enforcers don't expect someone to come toward the screaming — everyone in this place runs away from it. You find Nila first. She's wedged behind a coolant pipe, shaking, her hydrospanner raised like a club. You scoop her up. Then you follow the sound of Khyyra's growling to the main pen, where two enforcers are standing over her with shock prods. Your first shot drops one. Your second sends the other running. Khyyra rises to her full height, bloody, trembling, magnificent, and looks at you with the pure distilled gratitude of a soldier who has been holding the line alone and finally, finally sees reinforcement. You free her. You free her children. You fight your way back to the ship with Nila in one arm, Khyyra at your back, and the entire compound collapsing into chaos behind you. Not everyone makes it out. But Nila makes it. Khyyra's children make it. And when you hit hyperspace, the cargo hold is full of people who are breathing free air because someone heard screaming and ran toward it instead of away. The ghost is gone. Whatever you are now has a name — Nila gave it to you, whispered in Durese while she fell asleep against Khyyra's fur: 'Chain-breaker.' It sounds better than ghost.

choices.label: I find Nila and get her to the ship — I owe her that much. But Khyyra and the cages are beyond what one person can do.
choices.outcome: You trace Nila's hiding spot by following the scratches her hydrospanner left on the corridor walls — she marked her path, clever child, like leaving breadcrumbs in a fairy tale about monsters. You find her shaking but unbroken, her enormous eyes dry because she's past crying. 'I heard them hurting the others,' she whispers. 'I know,' you say. You carry her to the ship. Fash has the engines warm. Khyyra's howling follows you all the way to the landing bay, and Nila covers her ears but she doesn't cover her eyes — she watches the corridor behind you, waiting for someone else to come running. No one does. You lift off with three people — Fash, Nila, and your own battered conscience. The asteroid shrinks. The sounds stop. In hyperspace, Nila finally unclenches her fists and you see the red marks where her nails dug into her palms. 'The Wookiee lady,' she says. 'She was trying to protect the little ones.' 'I know,' you say again. 'Will someone come for her?' You don't answer. You set course for the outer rim and hold Nila's hand and try not to hear the echo of shock prods through walls you chose not to breach. Three saved. The rest — the screaming, the howling, the children Khyyra was shielding — they stay behind with the math you can't make work.

choices.label: I keep walking to the ship. The screaming isn't my fault. I didn't put those people in cages and I'm not responsible for getting them out.
choices.outcome: You walk. Sixty meters. Fash is already on the ramp. The engines hum. Behind you, the compound echoes with sounds that will follow you into hyperspace and beyond — a Wookiee's howl of defiance, a shock prod's electric snarl, and somewhere in the shadows, the silence of a ten-year-old who is hiding alone with a hydrospanner and learning, right now, that the person who looked her in the eye and walked away is the same as all the others. You fly. Fash gives you the data. The mission is complete. Clean extraction. Ghost protocol. Vel Drenn is satisfied. Your reputation is intact. And in the quiet hours, in the berths between jumps, in the moments when the ship is dark and still, you hear it. Not the screaming — that fades. What doesn't fade is the silence. Nila's silence when she watched you leave. Khyyra's silence between the shocks. The silence of the frequency your mother taught you — three short, two long, one short — a code that means 'family in trouble' — and you realize that somewhere, deep in the machinery of who you are, a cage just closed. Not a real one. Something worse. The kind you carry inside you. The kind that has no lock and no bars and no Duros girl with a hydrospanner to pry it open. Ghost protocol. You're alone in the dark. Just like you planned.

resolutions.light: You emerge from the Trandoshan compound carrying more weight than any ghost should bear. Your ship groans, your cover is shattered, and your body is bruised and bleeding. Every slaver network in the sector has your face now. You are known. You are hunted. But behind you, cages stand empty — not some of them, all of them — and a Duros girl who broke out with a hydrospanner sleeps in your co-pilot seat, and a Wookiee soldier who held the line alone has finally set down her guard. The Unchained doesn't calculate. It doesn't negotiate. It looks at chains and says: not today, not these, not while I have hands to break them. Vel Drenn calls you reckless. Nila calls you chain-breaker. Khyyra doesn't call you anything — she just places her hand flat against the bulkhead, the old Wookiee gesture that means 'I would follow you into the dark.' You became something the Trandoshans have never seen: a ghost who walked into the light and refused to go back.
resolutions.dark: You emerge from the run with your ship intact, your identity clean, and exactly the people you chose to carry. Fash is patched up, the data is secure, and your reputation as a professional who gets in and gets out without complications is reinforced. Grashk's compound hums along behind you, cages full, business uninterrupted. You completed the transaction. Vel Drenn is pleased. The credits are good. And in the cockpit of your ship, in the silence between stars, you listen for a sound that isn't there — a child's hand on cage bars, a Wookiee's growl of defiance, a frequency your mother taught you that nobody is broadcasting anymore. Ghost protocol kept you alive through the Purge and it kept you efficient tonight. The Transaction closes the books and walks away clean. But the ghost has learned something new: that silence has a weight, and cages have echoes, and some chains — the ones you carry inside, the ones you chose not to break — don't have locks. They have mirrors.
resolutions.neutral: You emerge from the compound carrying compromises in both hands. Some cages opened. Some didn't. Some children were saved by courage; some by commerce; some not at all. Nila is with you, or she isn't, but her face is burned into your memory either way — the red eyes, the hydrospanner, the quiet intensity of a child who learned to rescue herself when no one else would. Khyyra's hand-sign stays with you: 'I can fight.' Two words from a warrior who asked for help and received — what? An answer? A partial one? The truth is, you did what the situation allowed. Not everything. Not nothing. The Hunted learned long ago that liberation is a spectrum, not a switch, and that the distance between opening all the cages and opening none of them is measured in choices that no one else will ever understand. You'll keep flying. You'll keep listening for that frequency. And the next time chains rattle in the dark, you'll make the same impossible arithmetic all over again — knowing that the answer is never clean, never complete, and never, ever finished.
